titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Fazal Sheikh photographs
titlestmt.author: Elizabeth Nosari

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday March 25, 2026 at 23:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The aerial landscape photographs (2019–2022) in this collection by visual artist Fazal Sheikh consist of medium format (16.5" x 23.5") pigmented inkjet prints capturing sites of extraction, industry, and environmental degradation in the Western United States and the geographic scale of these lasting impacts on Indigenous lands.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from artist Fazal Sheikh on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2019–2022.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Fazal Sheikh photographs is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the author or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fazal Sheikh (b. 1965) is a visual artist who works primarily in the medium of photography. He was born and raised in New York City and also spent time in Kenya where his father's family emigrated from Northern India (present-day Pakistan). He graduated from Princeton University in 1987 with a bachelor of arts in fine art and art history.

Sheikh documents marginalized or underrepresented communities as well as the natural landscapes of their lived environments. He is perhaps best known for his portraits of refugees and migrants displaced by war and famine in regions around the world, including East Africa, Pakistan, Afghanistan, Brazil, Cuba, and India. In addition to photography, his artistic practice has also included personal narratives, sound recordings, and his own writing. His work is characterized by its emphasis on contextual research and collaboration, prioritizing building relationships with indigenous communities and researching their experiences. He also often explores the relationship between text and image and has collaborated with writers on publications such as Fazal Sheikh: Portraits (with Eduardo Cadava), 2011; Fazal Sheikh & Terry Tempest Williams: The Moon Is Behind Us , 2021; and Fazal Sheikh & Teju Cole: Human Archipelago , 2022.

Sheikh's work can be found in the collections of institutions worldwide, including the Stedelijk Museum, Amsterdam, The Netherlands; Art Institute of Chicago, Illinois; J. Paul Getty Museum, Los Angeles, California; National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.; National Museum of Kenya, Nairobi; and the Henri Cartier-Bresson Foundation, Paris, France. He has had solo exhibitions at the Museum of Contemporary Art, Moscow, Russia, 2004; Al-Ma'mal Foundation for Contemporary Art, East Jerusalem, Israeli-occupied Palestinian territory, 2016; and the Denver Art Museum, Denver, Colorado, 2024, among others. He has held fellowships, including the J. William Fulbright Foundation Fellowship, 1992; National Endowment for the Arts Fellowship, 1994; MacArthur Fellows Program, 2005; and John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation Fellowship, 2012. Sheikh has also been recognized with awards such as the International Center of Photography's Infinity Award, 1995; Le Prix Dialogue de l'Humanité, Recontres d'Arles, France, 2003; International Henri Cartier-Bresson Grand Prize, France, 2005; and Lucie Foundation Humanitarian Award, 2009.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Dates refer to the date of printing.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Fazal Sheikh Photographs, Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged in the order they were received.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Terry Tempest Williams Photography Collection (WA Photos 1348). Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Consult Access Services for access to the PDF file box list for this collection. This box list supplements the finding aid and includes a thumbnail image of each photographic print along with any associated associated metadata (e.g., date of printing, place of printing, and artist's inventory number). The photograph location has also been included wherever it has been identified.
The first folder in each box contains a printed version of the PDF file box list.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The aerial landscape photographs (2019–2022) in this collection by visual artist Fazal Sheikh consist of medium format (16.5" x 23.5") pigmented inkjet prints capturing sites of extraction, industry, and environmental degradation in the Western United States and the geographic scale of these lasting impacts on Indigenous lands. Sites include the Mexican Hat Uranium Mill Disposal Cell, Mexican Hat, Utah, in the Four Corners Region; Ambrosia Lake Uranium Disposal Cell, Grants Region, New Mexico; El Segundo Coal Mine, McKinley County, New Mexico; and U.S. Magnesium, Great Salt Lake, Utah, among others.
The locations of the photographs have been included wherever they have been identified. Dates refer to the date of printing.

did.unittitle: Inkjet prints (10), including of El Segundo Coal Mine in New Mexico

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Item 2 (00824) is a photograph of El Segundo Coal Mine, McKinley County, New Mexico.

did.unittitle: Inkjet prints (10), including of Mexican Hat Uranium Mill Disposal Cell and U.S. Magnesium in Utah

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Item 6 (01303) is a photograph of Mexican Hat Uranium Mill Disposal Cell, Mexican Hat, Utah.
Item 8 (00779) is a photograph of U.S. Magnesium, Great Salt Lake, Utah.

did.unittitle: Inkjet prints (10), including of the Great Salt Lake in Utah

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Item 10 (00525) is a photograph of the Great Salt Lake, Utah.

did.unittitle: Inkjet prints (10), including of Ambrosia Lake Uranium Disposal Cell in New Mexico

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Item 1 (00921) is a photograph of Ambrosia Lake Uranium Disposal Cell, Grants Region, New Mexico.
